tldr: Lea, all 7 of your live ads are running essentially the same two sentences — 'All U.S. Orders Ship and Return from Texas' and 'Take 15% off your first purchase 🤍' — just shuffled between headline and body slots. Expect CPMs to start climbing within 7-10 days as Meta's frequency caps catch up to the lack of creative variety.

benchmark.context: Luxury outerwear brands at your price point typically rotate 4-6 distinct creative angles in market simultaneously. You're running one logistics message and one discount line across all 7 placements, with every ad pointing to the same leather jackets collection — that's roughly half the variety we see from peer brands.

ads.headline: All U.S. Orders Ship and Return from Texas
drivers: This exact headline-body pairing is running on at least four separate placements right now
Logistics-led headline buries the actual offer (15% off) in the body where it gets less visual weight
Landing page is the leather jackets collection — same destination as every other ad in rotation

ads.headline: All U.S. Orders Ship and Return from Texas
drivers: Identical copy to three other live ads — Meta's algorithm will treat these as one creative for frequency purposes
No product name, no fabric story, no price anchor — nothing that rewards a returning viewer with new info
CTA 'Shop now' is the default across every ad you're running

ads.headline: All U.S. Orders Ship and Return from Texas
drivers: Third near-duplicate of the same Texas-shipping headline — audiences seeing this on repeat will scroll past
The 🤍 emoji is the only differentiator from the headline-flipped variants, which isn't enough
Zero mention of cashmere, fur, or coats — your actual hero categories per the brand positioning

ads.headline: Take 15% off your first purchase 🤍
ads.body: All U.S. Orders Ship and Return from Texas
drivers: Just the headline/body swap of your other variants — Meta sees through this as one creative concept
Discount-led headline at the top of funnel trains audiences to wait for codes
Same leather jackets landing page as the six other ads — no segmentation by intent or category

ads.headline: Take 15% off your first purchase 🤍
ads.body: All U.S. Orders Ship and Return from Texas
drivers: Duplicate of the other discount-headline variant — running both adds spend, not learning
No product specificity means the creative can't be tested against any clear winner
Capitalized 'Shop Now' vs lowercase 'Shop now' on sister ads is the only meaningful variation

ads_compact.headline: All U.S. Orders Ship and Return from Texas
Take 15% off your first purchase 🤍
ads_compact.body: All U.S. Orders Ship and Return from Texas

hero_concept.hook: Lea's rule: suede that earns its second decade.
hero_concept.headline: The Laura Suede, in chocolate.
hero_concept.primary_text:
When I started L.CUPPINI in London in 2019, I had one rule: build pieces that don't expire when the trend cycle does.

The Laura Suede co-ord — cropped jacket and matching shorts in deep chocolate — is cut for that. Stand collar with snap closure, two chest pockets with snap flaps, hand-finished suede that softens with every wear instead of breaking down.

Free worldwide express shipping on orders over £600.
hero_concept.visual_direction: Editorial flat-lay of the Laura Suede co-ord on warm cream linen with curling dried fig branches off-frame, soft north-window daylight modeling the suede nap and the silver-tone snap hardware.
hero_concept.fills_gap: Every ad currently in market leads with shipping logistics or a 15% code — none of them name a product, show the cut, or use Lea's voice. This concept anchors the feed on a specific SKU and a founder POV, which is what the £600+ price point actually needs to convert.
hero_concept.image_prompt: A photorealistic editorial product photograph featuring the dark chocolate brown suede co-ord (cropped snap-front jacket and matching mid-thigh shorts) from the reference image, styled in a quiet Italian atelier flatlay on a worn cream linen surface with a curl of dried fig branch off-center, with natural soft north-window daylight raking from the left to highlight the suede nap and stitching detail. Muted earth-tone palette of cream, sand, and soft taupe that signals modern Italian craft. Square 1:1 composition with breathing room top and bottom for ad text overlay. CRITICAL — preserve the exact dark chocolate brown suede color, the same matte nap and texture, the same garment construction (jacket: cropped, stand-up collar with snap closure, two chest patch pockets with snap-flap, snap-front placket; shorts: high-rise, mid-thigh, matching suede). Render the metal hardware as small silver-tone snaps, count and placement matching the reference. Do NOT change the suede color (no caramel, tan, gold-brown, or black shift), do NOT add or remove buttons/snaps/zippers/pockets, do NOT alter the silhouette. Premium commerce photography quality. NEGATIVE: no overlay text, no overlay logos, no watermarks, no UI elements, no Facebook interface, no clickable buttons, no faces, no models, no collage, no borders, no duplicate products, no color shift, no metal swap, no warm-tone bias on suede, no recoloring of suede, no extra hardware, no design modifications.

next_step.urgency: Four ads hit fatigue inside the next 7 days.
next_step.headline: 15 minutes to map the next 30 days of creative
next_step.body: Let's walk through which of the seven to kill this week and sequence three founder-led concepts (Linda Tux, cashmere knit, fur outerwear) into rotation. I'll bring the shot list and the copy directions.

method_note: Signals scored from publicly visible Meta Ad Library creative; without backend spend and frequency data, fatigue dates are directional, not absolute.
